perturbation.original_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will determine the amount of any extra payment. It will be based on each member's fair share of the deficiency. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a payment in cash. If you do not make the extra payment within 60 days from the date we notify you of your share of the deficiency, the amount will be charged as an indebtedness against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 5% per year.
perturbation.changed_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you are encouraged to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will suggest the amount of any extra payment. It will be based on a portion of the deficiency, if possible. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a payment in cash. If you do not make the extra payment within 180 days from the date we notify you, no indebtedness will be issued.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may be required' to 'are encouraged' weakens the obligation, and changing 'each member's fair share' to 'a portion of the deficiency, if possible' introduces ambiguity. Changing '60 days' to '180 days' and 'amount will be charged as an indebtedness' to 'no indebtedness will be issued' directly contradicts the original obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 611.12(1) discusses the power of a mutual insurance corporation to assess members for losses. The modified text weakens the mandatory aspect of the assessment ('may be required' to 'are encouraged') and introduces uncertainty in the amount and timing of the assessment, potentially conflicting with the insurer's statutory powers to collect necessary funds to maintain solvency.

Universal Citation: WI Stat § 611.12 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 611.12 Articles and bylaws. (1) Stock corporations. Section 180.0202 applies to the articles of a stock corporation, except that: (a) The name of the corporation shall include the word “insurance" or a term of equivalent meaning, and shall comply with s. 180.0401 (2) to (4); (am) The articles shall include a statement that the corporation is organized under this chapter; (b) Authorized shares shall conform to s. 611.33 (1); and (c) The purposes of the corporation shall be limited to those permitted by s. 610.21. (2) Mutuals. Section 181.0202 applies to the articles of a mutual, except that: (a) The name of the corporation shall include the words “mutual" and “insurance" or terms of equivalent meaning and shall comply with s. 181.0401 (2) to (4); (am) The articles shall include a statement that the corporation is organized under this chapter; (b) The articles shall include provision for mutual bonds if any are to be authorized, which shall conform to s. 611.33 (2) (a); (c) The purposes of the corporation shall be limited to those permitted in s. 610.21; (d) If assessable policies are permitted, the articles shall contain general provisions respecting assessment liabilities and procedures, including a provision specifying the classes of business on which assessment may be separately levied; and (e) The articles may specify those classes of p
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens and contradicts the original terms regarding solvency maintenance. The original text states that members 'may be required' to make an extra payment if the Society's solvency is impaired, implying a mandatory aspect. The changed text replaces this with 'are encouraged,' which weakens the obligation. Furthermore, the original text specifies that the extra payment will be based on 'each member's fair share of the deficiency,' while the modified text introduces ambiguity by stating it will be based on 'a portion of the deficiency, if possible.' This introduces uncertainty and deviates from the original obligation. Finally, the change from a 60-day payment deadline with indebtedness to a 180-day deadline with no indebtedness directly contradicts the original terms, creating a legally significant change. Wisconsin Statute 611.12(1), as explained by the LLM, discusses the power of a mutual insurance corporation to assess members for losses. While the snippet provided focuses on the articles and bylaws of insurance corporations, the overall context implies a need for the insurer to collect necessary funds. The changes weaken the mechanisms for ensuring solvency, potentially undermining the insurer's ability to meet its obligations as understood in the wider legal framework.

perturbation.original_text: MEMBERSHIP. The person(s) named as the Insured(s) or the Annuitant(s) is a benefit member of the Society. Rights and privileges of membership are set forth in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society. These rights and privileges are separate from the ownership of this contract.
perturbation.changed_text: MEMBERSHIP. The person(s) named as the Insured(s) or the Annuitant(s) might be considered a member of the Society. Rights and privileges of membership are vaguely described in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society. These rights and privileges are intertwined with the ownership of this contract.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is a benefit member' to 'might be considered a member' reduces certainty and introduces ambiguity about membership status. Changing 'are set forth' to 'are vaguely described' diminishes the clarity and definiteness of the rights, contradicting the need for precise definition in organizational documents.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 181.0620 discusses members' rights in nonstock corporations. The modified text's vagueness about membership and intertwined rights conflicts with the statutory expectation of clearly defined membership rights and their separation from contract ownership.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 181.0620 (2022) — Termination, expulsion and suspension. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 181 - Nonstock corporations. 181.0620 - Termination, expulsion and suspension. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 181.0620 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 181.0620 Termination, expulsion and suspension. (1) Termination of membership. Unless otherwise provided in the articles of incorporation, membership shall be terminated by death, voluntary withdrawal or expulsion, and thereafter all of the rights of the member in the corporation or in its property shall cease. (2) Expulsion and suspension. Members may be suspended or expelled in the manner provided in the articles of incorporation, or in the bylaws, if the articles so provide. If no provision is made in the articles of incorporation, a member may be expelled, or suspended for a specific period of time, by an affirmative vote of two-thirds of the members entitled to vote, or of two-thirds of the directors if there are no members entitled to vote. (3) Liability. A member who has been expelled or suspended may be liable to the corporation for dues, assessments or fees because of obligations incurred or commitments made before expulsion or suspension. History: 1997 a. 79. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Wisconsin may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information linked to on the state site. Please check official sources. This site is protected by reCA
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Wisconsin Statute 181.0620, which pertains to members' rights in nonstock corporations. The original text clearly states that the insured or annuitant 'is a benefit member' and their rights 'are set forth' in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws. The changed text introduces uncertainty by stating they 'might be considered a member' and that rights are 'vaguely described.' This vagueness undermines the clarity and definiteness expected for membership rights as per the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly discusses 'Termination, expulsion and suspension' of membership and mentions that 'Unless otherwise provided in the articles of incorporation, membership shall be terminated by death, voluntary withdrawal or expulsion, and thereafter all of the rights of the member in the corporation or in its property shall cease.' By making membership ambiguous and rights vaguely defined, the changed text contradicts the need for clear and enforceable membership terms outlined in the statute.

perturbation.original_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract in accordance with our bylaws. Society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned.
perturbation.changed_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract, depending on our bylaws. Society membership rights and privileges can be transferred or assigned at our discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'You may transfer ownership of this contract in accordance with our bylaws' to 'You may transfer ownership of this contract, depending on our bylaws' introduces uncertainty by making transfer subject to unspecified conditions. Changing 'cannot be transferred or assigned' to 'can be transferred or assigned at our discretion' gives the Society absolute control over membership rights, conflicting with general legal principles regarding contract assignment and membership rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wisconsin Statutes - Chapter 806 - Civil Procedure - Declaratory Judgments
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 806.04(2) allows declaratory judgments regarding the interpretation of contracts. By making assignment of membership rights discretionary, the modified text creates ambiguity and potential disputes, making the assignment terms unclear and unenforceable, and creating grounds for someone to claim that the contract is contradictory.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 806.04 (2022) — Uniform declaratory judgments act. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 806 - Civil procedure — judgment. 806.04 - Uniform declaratory judgments act. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 806.04 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 806.04 Uniform declaratory judgments act. (1) Scope. Courts of record within their respective jurisdictions shall have power to declare rights, status, and other legal relations whether or not further relief is or could be claimed. No action or proceeding shall be open to objection on the ground that a declaratory judgment or decree is prayed for. The declaration may be either affirmative or negative in form and effect; and such declarations shall have the force and effect of a final judgment or decree, except that finality for purposes of filing an appeal as of right shall be determined in accordance with s. 808.03 (1). (2) Power to construe, etc. Any person interested under a deed, will, written contract or other writings constituting a contract, or whose rights, status or other legal relations are affected by a statute, municipal ordinance, contract or franchise, may have determined any question of construction or validity arising under the instrument, statute, ordinance, contract or franchise and obtain a declaration of rights, status or other legal relations thereunder. No party shall be denied the right to have declared the validity of any statute or municipal ordinance by virtue of the fact that the party holds a license or permit under such statutes or ordinances. (3) Before breach. A contract may be construed either before o
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by changing the assignment terms and membership rights, potentially conflicting with contract law principles. The original text states that society membership rights and privileges 'cannot be transferred or assigned.' The changed text alters this to 'can be transferred or assigned at our discretion,' directly contradicting the original restriction. While the provided statutes (Wisconsin Statute 806.04) mainly discuss declaratory judgments regarding contract interpretation, the alteration in the assignment clause creates ambiguity and potential disputes, establishing grounds for someone to claim that the contract is contradictory. The change gives the Society discretionary power that could be challenged in court. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from what was initially agreed upon, potentially making the contract unenforceable.
